title: introduce-policy-contracts-kms-commitment-ledger-and-residency-gate
preview: Context and motivation: Customers operating under jurisdictional controls asked for stronger, auditable guarantees that (1) requests that claim a region-bound residency are actually served with region-anchored CMKs, (2) operator principles cannot bypass tenant-scoped RBAC policies, and (3) audit trails contain verifiable cryptographic markers tying requests to KMS commitments. This PR implements three coordinated pieces: (A) a lightweight Policy Contracts DSL and compiler that lets product and compliance own high-level contracts (e.g., "Tenant must use CMK in eu-west-1 for classification=restricted") and compiles into RBACv2 assertions and enforcer rules; (B) a KMS Commitment Ledger service that records authoritative, append-only KMS assignment decisions per request (signed by the enforcer) and surfaces a compact proof token that is attached to audit events; (C) a Residency Gate enforcement shim that runs in the mux layer to block/redirect requests that violate region pin constraints and surface clear error codes for callers. Implementation summary: - New package rbac-policy-bundle/policy_contracts: DSL spec (YAML first-class), compiler, and unit tests; includes example contracts for tenant residency, operator-scoped denylists, and service-level allowances. - Enforcer integration: authz/enforcer now consumes compiled policy contracts at startup (and supports runtime refresh). When the enforcer binds a KMS choice to a request it issues a KMS Commitment entry (ledger client) and returns a compact commitment token (base64url) which is attached to the request context. - Commitment ledger: services/commitment-ledger introduces a small append-only service with a signing key (rotatable via KMS). Entries include request_id, chosen_kms_key_id, region, policy_contract_id, signer_kid, and signature. Ledger exposes a read-only API for auditors and an expiry-based retention policy configured per-tenant. - Audit enrichment: audit/forwarder picks up commitment tokens and expands them into commitment metadata (last 8 chars of signer_kid, region, contract id hash) while preserving the original event. Enriched audit records pass through existing pipelines unchanged for consumers that don't need the extra fields. - Residency Gate: ingress/mux/residency_gate implements a fast path check for region assertions and KMS compatibility matrix. Failing checks return 403 with an explicit policy_violation code and a link to remediation docs. - Migration & rollout: feature flag rbac_policy_contracts.enabled with staged rollout; sticky migration script migrates a small cohort of tenants to the new enforcement path; compatibility shim ensures legacy bindings still resolve to prior KMS routing if the tenant is not migrated. Commits (high-level): - add(policy_contracts): DSL + compiler + examples - feat(enforcer): runtime contract loader + ledger client - feat(ledger): append-only commitment store + signer integration - feat(audit): enrich forwarder with commitment tokens - feat(mux): residency gate enforcement + error codes - docs: operator migration guide + consumer audit docs - tests: unit + integration + one flake-fix commit Tests and CI: - Unit test coverage added for DSL compiler, ledger signing and verification, and residency gate logic. - Integration tests include a simulated tenant migration with an in-memory ledger. - CI matrix: linux/amd64 unit, integration:e2e, lint, go-sec-scan. One flaky test (audit/forwarder TestBatchFlush) was fixed by replacing time.Sleep with a condition variable; CI green on rerun. Files changed (high-level): - rbac-policy-bundle/policy_contracts/{spec.md,schema.yaml,compiler.go,compiler_test.go,examples/tenant_residency.yaml} - authz/enforcer/{loader.go,binding_resolver.go,ledger_client.go} - services/commitment-ledger/{server.go,store.go,signer.go,api.md} - audit/forwarder/{enricher.go,enricher_test.go} - ingress/mux/{residency_gate.go,residency_gate_test.go} - docs/compliance/policy-contracts.md - installer/migrations/{migrate_policy_contracts.go} - charts/rbac-policy-bundle/values.yaml (feature flag) Review and feedback (selected): - Priya Rao (reviewer): "Can we avoid storing full key material in the ledger? auditors only need key id and region plus signed assertion." Author reply: "We only store key id + region + signed assertion; comments updated to clarify and added storage schema test." - Marta Alvarez: "We need explicit TTL and retention config per tenant for privacy/legal requests." Author reply: "Added tenant_retention_days in ledger config; added a migration that backfills TTL for existing tenants in the cohort." - Liam O'Connell: "How do we ensure operator-scoped roles can't escalate via policy contracts?" Author reply: "Policy contracts compile into RBACv2 constraints that are enforced in the PDP; operator roles are evaluated with a deny-first merge. Added unit tests covering operator override attempts." - Security review note (from sec-eng): "Signer key rotation must be audited; document added and rotation integrated into KMS provisioning flow." Author reply: "Rotation docs and a rotation e2e added; ledger exposes signer metadata in /v1/status." Billing/usage & telemetry: - Added metrics: policy_contracts.compiled, ledger.append.success, ledger.append.fail, residency_gate.denied, residency_gate.allowed. - Added trace spans for ledger-sign and commitment-attach with sampling tag policy_contract=true. Migration plan (operator-facing): - Phase 0: feature flag off. - Phase 1: enable read-only contract compilation and dry-run logging for 1% tenants. - Phase 2: enable enforcement for a small cohort; monitor metric residency_gate.denied and audit enrichment volume. - Phase 3: expand to 50% then 100% and deprecate legacy shim. Backout plan included. Observability: dashboards added in docs/dashboards/policy_contracts.json and alert policy: residency_gate.denied_rate > 0.1% sustained triggers P1. Security and compliance considerations: - Ledger entries are signed; access to ledger read API is restricted to compliance personas; retention configurable per-tenant; access logs forwarded to audit pipeline. - No plaintext key material stored. - Contract DSL reviewed for injection/resolution attack surfaces; compiler sandboxed. Docs and operator guide: - docs/compliance/policy-contracts.md with example workflows and remediation steps. - docs/upgrade/migration_steps.md with CLI helpers. Merge and rollout: - Merged via squash and merge after approvals. Merge commit: "Introduce policy contracts, commitment ledger, and residency gate (squash)". Notes and follow-ups: - Follow-up tasks added: ENG-8810 (policy-explain endpoint), SEC-345 (attestation of ledger signer), PM-402 enable customer-facing contract templates. - Suggested future work: compact zk-friendly proof for commitments; further KMS multi-provider cross-checks. Overall impact: This change centralizes compliance guardrails into an auditable, reviewable contract model that ties RBACv2 assertions to KMS choices and audit entries. It provides a clear, staged migration path and operator-facing controls required for region-pin and residency SLAs.

title: Add Optimization Config change permissions + operator roles (apply/promote/rollback)
preview: Summary: Introduces explicit RBAC permissions for the Optimization Config lifecycle (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback) and adds two opinionated roles used by Console + control plane workflows. This is part of the Safe apply + rollback mechanism project to ensure rollback is both auditable and access-controlled. Motivation: Today, optimization config changes are implicitly covered by broad “platform operator” capabilities in some environments, and not consistently modeled in Private deployments. Enterprise customers and Security review require least-privilege roles with clear separation between “can propose” and “can execute rollback in prod.” Changes included: 1) New permissions: optimization_config.read, optimization_config.create_version, optimization_config.validate, optimization_config.diff, optimization_config.apply, optimization_config.promote, optimization_config.rollback, optimization_config.override_gates (break-glass), optimization_config.manage_rbac (internal only). 2) New roles: optimization_change_operator (intended for on-call + platform operators executing safe apply/rollback within an approved scope; includes read/diff/validate/apply/rollback; does NOT include promote-by-default to reduce accidental broad rollouts), optimization_change_admin (intended for senior operators; includes promote + override_gates; still does not include manage_rbac). 3) Updates to policy docs: clarified mapping from Console actions (“Rollback” button, “Promote to production”, “Override gates”) to permissions and recommended reviewer/approval process. 4) Tests: added rego/policy tests + golden snapshots covering allow/deny cases for Hosted/Dedicated/Private personas, including a regression test for INT-3390 (operator could not see rollback button due to missing permission). Backwards compatibility: No default widening of access; existing roles are unchanged except for documentation noting recommended migration. Control plane/Console will explicitly request these permissions when the feature flag is enabled. Rollout plan: Land policy bundle first; then enable in staging; then gate Console button visibility on optimization_config.rollback + server-side authz checks in control plane. Notes: “override_gates” is explicitly separate and intended for internal break-glass flows only (guarded by additional approval and audited separately).

Control plane/Console will explicitly request these permissions when the feature flag is enabled. Notes: “override_gates” is explicitly separate and intended for internal break-glass flows only (guarded by additional approval and audited separately).

title: Add break-glass verification override guardrails + required audit annotations
preview: Title: Add break-glass verification override guardrails + required audit annotations. Context: As part of offline bundle signature verification, the installer now supports a policy-gated break-glass flag (see redwood-private-installer pr-24979). This PR adds cluster-side guardrails so that (1) break-glass cannot be enabled silently, (2) use is attributable and exportable via audit logs, and (3) only approved operators can authorize it. Summary of changes: (A) Adds a Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplate + Constraint enforcing required annotations whenever break-glass is enabled via the Redwood Private install metadata ConfigMap (redwood-private/install-metadata) or Helm values rendered into that ConfigMap. Required annotations: redwood.ai/break-glass-ticket (SUP-/INT-/customer ticket id), redwood.ai/break-glass-reason (freeform, min 20 chars), redwood.ai/break-glass-approved-by (corporate email), redwood.ai/break-glass-approved-at (RFC3339 timestamp), redwood.ai/break-glass-expires-at (RFC3339; must be <= 72h from approved-at). Optional but recommended: redwood.ai/break-glass-approver-role (Security|ReleaseEng|SRE). (B) Adds a second constraint that denies enabling break-glass unless the requesting subject is in the allowlisted RBAC group (redwood:break-glass-approvers) or is a ServiceAccount with label redwood.ai/purpose=private-installer and bound to ClusterRole redwood-private-break-glass-approver. (C) Adds a policy exception mechanism for dev/test clusters only: if Namespace has label redwood.ai/environment=dev and redwood.ai/policy-exceptions contains break-glass-guardrails, constraints run in "warn" mode and include a warning message; default remains "deny" for all other environments. (D) Adds a small conftest test suite covering: missing annotations, malformed timestamps, expiry > 72h, approver not allowlisted, and the dev warn-only path. Why this approach: We want verification to fail closed by default but still support emergency installs when signatures cannot be verified due to environmental constraints (time skew, trust store mismatch, etc.). The installer already logs local audit markers; these policies ensure the cluster also has consistent, queryable attribution for any break-glass activation, and prevent accidental enablement by automation. Implementation notes: Policies target the install-metadata ConfigMap because it is the canonical place we already read for support bundles and audit export mapping. We intentionally avoid requiring outbound connectivity or external approvals (air-gapped), but we require the metadata fields that support and security will request during incident response and compliance evidence collection. Files / areas changed: policy/gatekeeper/templates/redwoodprivatebreakglass_annotations_template.yaml; policy/gatekeeper/constraints/redwoodprivatebreakglass_annotations_constraint.yaml; policy/gatekeeper/templates/redwoodprivatebreakglass_approver_template.yaml; policy/gatekeeper/constraints/redwoodprivatebreakglass_approver_constraint.yaml; rbac/clusterroles/redwood-private-break-glass-approver.yaml; rbac/clusterrolebindings/redwood-private-break-glass-approver-binding.yaml; tests/conftest/break_glass_annotations_test.rego; tests/fixtures/install-metadata-configmap.yaml; README.md (break-glass section). Illustrative snippet (ConstraintTemplate logic, shortened): "violation[{"msg": msg}] { input.review.kind.kind == "ConfigMap"; input.review.object.metadata.name == "install-metadata"; input.review.object.metadata.namespace == "redwood-private"; input.review.object.data["break_glass_enabled"] == "true"; missing := {"redwood.ai/break-glass-ticket", "redwood.ai/break-glass-reason", "redwood.ai/break-glass-approved-by", "redwood.ai/break-glass-approved-at", "redwood.ai/break-glass-expires-at"} - {k | input.review.object.metadata.annotations[k]}; count(missing) > 0; msg := sprintf("break-glass enabled but missing required annotations: %v", [missing]) }". Testing: make policy-lint; make test-conftest; validated against a local kind cluster with Gatekeeper v3.14. CI: gatekeeper-schema-check, conftest, yamllint all green. Rollout: This is a no-op unless Gatekeeper is installed (standard for regulated Private installs). For existing clusters, apply templates/constraints first (will only affect future changes to install metadata). Related: ENG-4824 (break-glass design), ENG-4829 (audit event schema), SUP-31977 (support guidance).

For existing clusters, apply templates/constraints first (will only affect future changes to install metadata). title: Add least-privilege RBAC for release signing service operator
preview:
## Summary
Introduce a dedicated, least-privilege RBAC role for "release signing operators" who need to initiate and monitor offline bundle signing runs for Redwood Private releases.

This PR removes the current ad-hoc guidance of granting broad permissions in the release-tools cluster and replaces it with an explicit, reviewable policy bundle:
- New `ClusterRole` + namespace-scoped bindings for `release-signing-operator`
- Separation-of-duties: operator can create/approve signing requests and observe jobs, but cannot read signing key material (Secrets) or mutate trust roots
- Policy tests to prevent accidental wildcard RBAC in this area

Context: offline bundle signing pipeline workstream (ENG-4822) + trust store/key rotation migration (ENG-4825). This is intended to be deployed alongside the signing service rollout in the release-tools cluster.

## Motivation / background
As part of on-prem offline artifact signing, we’re introducing a signing service that produces detached signatures for offline bundles. The human/operator flow is: start a signing run, monitor status, and fetch resulting artifacts from the artifact store. Historically this has been done with elevated cluster access (sometimes effectively admin in `release-tools`), which is incompatible with our supply-chain threat model and the evidence expectations we’ve seen from air-gapped/regulated customers.

Threat model alignment:
- Reduce blast radius of compromised operator credentials
- Ensure operators cannot access private key material or modify signing configuration/trust roots
- Improve auditability: explicit role name and bindings show up cleanly in access reviews

## What changed
### New RBAC objects
- `clusters/production/release-tools/rbac/release-signing-operator.clusterrole.yaml`
- `clusters/production/release-tools/rbac/release-signing-operator.rolebinding.yaml`
- `clusters/production/release-tools/rbac/release-signing-operator.serviceaccount.yaml`

Permissions granted (high level):
- Create/update/get/list/watch `signingrequests.signing.redwood.ai` (CRD) in `release-signing` namespace
- Get/list/watch Jobs/Pods in `release-signing` namespace for run status
- Get/list Events in `release-signing` namespace
- Read ConfigMaps used for non-secret job parameters

Explicitly NOT granted:
- No `secrets` access (get/list/watch)
- No write access to ConfigMaps that define trust roots (`signing-trust-*`)
- No cluster-scoped writes beyond the specific CRD

### Removed/cleaned up
- Dropped legacy `release-operator` binding that included broad verbs over core resources in the namespace.

### Policy/CI guardrails
- Add `conftest` rules to fail on:
  - Wildcard resources/verbs in signing operator roles
  - Any Secrets access from roles matching `*signing*operator*`

## Example manifests (illustrative)
`release-signing-operator.clusterrole.yaml` (excerpt)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: release-signing-operator
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["signing.redwood.ai"]
    resources: ["signingrequests"]
    verbs: ["create", "get", "list", "watch", "update", "patch"]
  - apiGroups: ["batch"]
    resources: ["jobs"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "events", "configmaps"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

`release-signing-operator.rolebinding.yaml` (excerpt)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: release-signing-operator
  namespace: release-signing
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: release-signing-operators
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: release-signing-operator
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

## Operational notes
- This PR assumes a distinct `release-signing` namespace (already present in release-tools).
- Group `release-signing-operators` maps to the subset of Release Engineering on-call who initiates signing runs.
- Security admins retain separate, tighter roles for key ceremony operations; those bindings are out of scope here.

## Rollout plan
1) Merge policy bundle
2) Apply to staging release-tools and validate:
   - Operator can submit SigningRequest
   - Operator cannot read any Secret in `release-signing`
3) Apply to prod release-tools
4) Update internal access request template to point to `release-signing-operator` (tracked in INT-11021)

## Testing
- `make lint` (kustomize + yaml lint)
- `make test` (conftest policy checks)
- `kustomize build clusters/production/release-tools | kubeconform` (schema validation)

## Release notes
- Security: add least-privilege RBAC role `release-signing-operator` for offline bundle signing operations in release-tools

---
### Commits
1. rbac: add release-signing-operator ClusterRole + bindings
2. rbac: remove legacy release-operator binding from release-signing namespace
3. policy: add conftest checks preventing wildcard + secrets access for signing operator roles
4. docs: add README note for requesting signing operator access

---
### Review conversation (abridged)
Marcus Lin (Security Eng): "Please ensure the operator role has *no* access to Secrets, including indirect access via `*` in core API groups. Also, can we avoid `update` on ConfigMaps to prevent trust-root drift?"
Devon Price (author): "Agreed. Removed any Secrets verbs (none added, but added conftest to block regressions). Kept ConfigMaps read-only and confirmed the trust-root configmaps are owned by a separate admin role."

Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): "We need to be able to watch Pods and Jobs to drive the signing run status UI + troubleshooting. `pods/log` would be useful, but I’m okay deferring if it creates risk."
Devon Price (author): "Added get/list/watch for Pods/Jobs/Events. Not adding `pods/log` in this PR; we can add a separate, scoped role if needed with explicit justification + audit expectations."

Sahana Iyer (Build/CI): "Conftest check failed on first pass because the rule matched the old role too. Looks good now after removing legacy binding."
Devon Price (author): "Fixed by deleting the legacy rolebinding and scoping the conftest selector to `release-signing-operator` resources."

Hanae Suzuki (KMS/Secrets): "Thanks for keeping Secrets out of operator path. Please also ensure there’s no access to `serviceaccounts/token` subresource." 
Devon Price (author): "Confirmed: no SA token subresource permissions. Added a small comment in the manifest to call this out."

Approvals:
- Marcus Lin: Approved
- Connor O'Brien: Approved
- Sahana Iyer: Approved

---
### CI status
- build/kustomize: PASS
- lint/yamllint: PASS
- policy/conftest: PASS (rerun after rule fix)
- validate/kubeconform: PASS

Merged via squash into `main`.
text:
## Summary
Introduce a dedicated, least-privilege RBAC role for "release signing operators" who need to initiate and monitor offline bundle signing runs for Redwood Private releases.

This PR removes the current ad-hoc guidance of granting broad permissions in the release-tools cluster and replaces it with an explicit, reviewable policy bundle:
- New `ClusterRole` + namespace-scoped bindings for `release-signing-operator`
- Separation-of-duties: operator can create/approve signing requests and observe jobs, but cannot read signing key material (Secrets) or mutate trust roots
- Policy tests to prevent accidental wildcard RBAC in this area

Context: offline bundle signing pipeline workstream (ENG-4822) + trust store/key rotation migration (ENG-4825). This is intended to be deployed alongside the signing service rollout in the release-tools cluster.

## Motivation / background
As part of on-prem offline artifact signing, we’re introducing a signing service that produces detached signatures for offline bundles. The human/operator flow is: start a signing run, monitor status, and fetch resulting artifacts from the artifact store. Historically this has been done with elevated cluster access (sometimes effectively admin in `release-tools`), which is incompatible with our supply-chain threat model and the evidence expectations we’ve seen from air-gapped/regulated customers.

Threat model alignment:
- Reduce blast radius of compromised operator credentials
- Ensure operators cannot access private key material or modify signing configuration/trust roots
- Improve auditability: explicit role name and bindings show up cleanly in access reviews

## What changed
### New RBAC objects
- `clusters/production/release-tools/rbac/release-signing-operator.clusterrole.yaml`
- `clusters/production/release-tools/rbac/release-signing-operator.rolebinding.yaml`
- `clusters/production/release-tools/rbac/release-signing-operator.serviceaccount.yaml`

Permissions granted (high level):
- Create/update/get/list/watch `signingrequests.signing.redwood.ai` (CRD) in `release-signing` namespace
- Get/list/watch Jobs/Pods in `release-signing` namespace for run status
- Get/list Events in `release-signing` namespace
- Read ConfigMaps used for non-secret job parameters

Explicitly NOT granted:
- No `secrets` access (get/list/watch)
- No write access to ConfigMaps that define trust roots (`signing-trust-*`)
- No cluster-scoped writes beyond the specific CRD

### Removed/cleaned up
- Dropped legacy `release-operator` binding that included broad verbs over core resources in the namespace.

### Policy/CI guardrails
- Add `conftest` rules to fail on:
  - Wildcard resources/verbs in signing operator roles
  - Any Secrets access from roles matching `*signing*operator*`

## Example manifests (illustrative)
`release-signing-operator.clusterrole.yaml` (excerpt)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: release-signing-operator
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["signing.redwood.ai"]
    resources: ["signingrequests"]
    verbs: ["create", "get", "list", "watch", "update", "patch"]
  - apiGroups: ["batch"]
    resources: ["jobs"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "events", "configmaps"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

`release-signing-operator.rolebinding.yaml` (excerpt)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: release-signing-operator
  namespace: release-signing
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: release-signing-operators
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: release-signing-operator
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

## Operational notes
- This PR assumes a distinct `release-signing` namespace (already present in release-tools).
- Group `release-signing-operators` maps to the subset of Release Engineering on-call who initiates signing runs.
- Security admins retain separate, tighter roles for key ceremony operations; those bindings are out of scope here.

## Rollout plan
1) Merge policy bundle
2) Apply to staging release-tools and validate:
   - Operator can submit SigningRequest
   - Operator cannot read any Secret in `release-signing`
3) Apply to prod release-tools
4) Update internal access request template to point to `release-signing-operator` (tracked in INT-11021)

## Testing
- `make lint` (kustomize + yaml lint)
- `make test` (conftest policy checks)
- `kustomize build clusters/production/release-tools | kubeconform` (schema validation)

## Release notes
- Security: add least-privilege RBAC role `release-signing-operator` for offline bundle signing operations in release-tools

---
### Commits
1. rbac: add release-signing-operator ClusterRole + bindings
2. rbac: remove legacy release-operator binding from release-signing namespace
3. policy: add conftest checks preventing wildcard + secrets access for signing operator roles
4. docs: add README note for requesting signing operator access

---
### Review conversation (abridged)
Marcus Lin (Security Eng): "Please ensure the operator role has *no* access to Secrets, including indirect access via `*` in core API groups. Also, can we avoid `update` on ConfigMaps to prevent trust-root drift?"
Devon Price (author): "Agreed. Removed any Secrets verbs (none added, but added conftest to block regressions). Kept ConfigMaps read-only and confirmed the trust-root configmaps are owned by a separate admin role."

Connor O'Brien (Release Eng): "We need to be able to watch Pods and Jobs to drive the signing run status UI + troubleshooting. `pods/log` would be useful, but I’m okay deferring if it creates risk."
Devon Price (author): "Added get/list/watch for Pods/Jobs/Events. Not adding `pods/log` in this PR; we can add a separate, scoped role if needed with explicit justification + audit expectations."

Sahana Iyer (Build/CI): "Conftest check failed on first pass because the rule matched the old role too. Looks good now after removing legacy binding."
Devon Price (author): "Fixed by deleting the legacy rolebinding and scoping the conftest selector to `release-signing-operator` resources."

Hanae Suzuki (KMS/Secrets): "Thanks for keeping Secrets out of operator path. Please also ensure there’s no access to `serviceaccounts/token` subresource." 
Devon Price (author): "Confirmed: no SA token subresource permissions. Added a small comment in the manifest to call this out."

Approvals:
- Marcus Lin: Approved
- Connor O'Brien: Approved
- Sahana Iyer: Approved

---
### CI status
- build/kustomize: PASS
- lint/yamllint: PASS
- policy/conftest: PASS (rerun after rule fix)
- validate/kubeconform: PASS

Merged via squash into `main`.

title: Add audit export permissions + default role mappings (enterprise admin vs auditor)
preview:
Summary
This updates the rbac-policy-bundle to include new permissions required for SIEM-ready audit export configuration + operational visibility. It also updates default role mappings to keep least-privilege separation between enterprise admins (manage exports) and auditors (read-only + status).

Context
The control plane and Console are introducing audit export CRUD + delivery status endpoints behind a beta feature flag. This PR updates the central policy bundle so permissions are standardized across Hosted and Dedicated. Private/on-prem roles are intentionally unchanged for beta.

Linked work
- ENG-5173 (RBAC permissions for export management)
- Control plane endpoints: redwood/pr-28792 (RBAC) + redwood/pr-28766 (config API) (FYI)

What changed
1) New permission set (names + semantics)
- audit_exports.read
  - Allows viewing export configurations (destination type, enabled/disabled, filter settings, backfill window) but not secrets.
- audit_exports.write
  - Allows creating/updating/deleting export configurations, enabling/disabling exports, and modifying filters/backfill controls.
- audit_exports.secrets.rotate
  - Allows rotating credential references (e.g., Splunk HEC token ref, Datadog API key ref, AWS role ARN / external ID ref). Does not grant read access to underlying secret material.
- audit_exports.status.read
  - Allows viewing operational status/health (last_success_at, last_error_at, export_lag_seconds, adapter error counters) surfaced in Console.

Notes on least-privilege
- Secrets are represented as references (vault path / secret manager ID) and never exposed as readable fields via RBAC in this bundle.
- audit_exports.write does not imply audit event read access.
- audit_exports.status.read is separated so auditors/SecOps can monitor health without config write privileges.

2) Default role mapping updates
- enterprise_admin (and legacy admin-equivalent roles)
  - Granted: audit_exports.read, audit_exports.write, audit_exports.secrets.rotate, audit_exports.status.read
- security_auditor
  - Granted: audit_exports.read, audit_exports.status.read
  - Not granted: audit_exports.write, audit_exports.secrets.rotate
- readonly / billing / developer roles
  - No change (no export management permissions added)

3) Policy docs / examples
- Added a short section to the role catalog describing audit export permissions and recommended assignment ("Admin owns configuration; Auditor owns monitoring + evidence"), plus a note that export delivery is at-least-once and downstream dedup is recommended.

Why this approach
- Matches separation-of-duties expectations from Security reviews (config vs oversight).
- Minimizes blast radius if an auditor token is compromised (can’t change destinations/filters or rotate creds).
- Keeps permission granularity aligned with control plane endpoint grouping.

Testing
- bundle lint/validate
- role compilation tests
- snapshot update for default roles

Rollout
- No runtime change; this is a policy bundle update.
- Control plane enforcement remains feature-flagged per tenant allowlist.

Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Add a dedicated "security_ops" role variant if we see customers wanting status.read without read of config details (currently status.read still requires config metadata for Console UX).
- Revisit permission naming consistency with other integrations (e.g., destinations.*) once API surface stabilizes.

Checklist
- [x] New permissions documented
- [x] Default role mappings updated
- [x] CI green
- [x] Reviewed with Security + Control Plane

Review notes / discussion
Ethan Park (requested changes, 2025-03-22): Can we split status read from config read? Console status page is going to call both endpoints; but conceptually we should keep them separate.
Author (reply): Added audit_exports.status.read as separate permission. Kept audit_exports.read required for config listing/details. status.read is now separate and can be granted independently. (Control plane already checks both as needed.)

Marcus Lin (requested changes, 2025-03-23): Please ensure auditors cannot rotate credentials and cannot enable/disable exports; also confirm no secret material is in any readable resource fields.
Author (reply): Confirmed. Rotation is gated by audit_exports.secrets.rotate. Enable/disable is treated as a config mutation under audit_exports.write. Policy bundle does not define any readable secret fields; all secrets are references.

Kevin Osei (comment, 2025-03-24): Add a short note in docs that retention is separate from export and that exports are best-effort with retry (at-least-once). We get SOC2 questionnaire confusion here.
Author (reply): Added doc note pointing to retention policy language (no net-new claims). Avoided over-specifying delivery semantics beyond at-least-once.

Benji Okafor (approval, 2025-03-26): Looks good; matches endpoint permission checks in redwood PR. +1.

CI
- policy-bundle / validate: PASS
- policy-bundle / lint: PASS
- policy-bundle / unit-tests: PASS
- release-dry-run: PASS

Merge
Squash merged to main on 2025-03-26.

Release notes
Enterprise: Introduces granular RBAC permissions for managing SIEM-ready audit export configurations and viewing exporter health/status (beta).

- [x] CI green

Author (reply): Confirmed. Rotation is gated by audit_exports.secrets.rotate. Enable/disable is treated as a config mutation under audit_exports.write. Policy bundle does not define any readable secret fields; all secrets are references.

Kevin Osei (comment, 2025-03-24): Add a short note in docs that retention is separate from export and that exports are best-effort with retry (at-least-once). We get SOC2 questionnaire confusion here.
Author (reply): Added doc note pointing to retention policy language (no net-new claims). Avoided over-specifying delivery semantics beyond at-least-once.

Benji Okafor (approval, 2025-03-26): Looks good; matches endpoint permission checks in redwood PR. +1.

CI
- policy-bundle / validate: PASS
- policy-bundle / lint: PASS
- policy-bundle / unit-tests: PASS
- release-dry-run: PASS

Merge
Squash merged to main on 2025-03-26.

Release notes
Enterprise: Introduces granular RBAC permissions for managing SIEM-ready audit export configurations and viewing exporter health/status (beta).

title: Add Private deployment operator + auditor role templates (env/cluster scoped)
preview: Summary: This PR adds a set of built-in RBAC v2 role templates intended for Redwood Private deployments, focused on common operator and auditor workflows. The templates are explicitly scoped (environment and cluster) and are designed to support least-privilege, separation of duties, and evidence-friendly auditability. Context/motivation: Private customers (and internal pilots) have asked for clear “day-2 operations” roles (upgrade, health checks, log viewing) and separate read-only/audit roles that can export audit logs without granting mutation privileges. We also saw multiple support cases where Dedicated/Private admins were blocked due to missing template permissions or where customers over-granted Org Admin to solve an operator need (SUP-48288, SUP-48219). This PR is part of ENG-18444 (role template generator + templates) and aligns with the scope model decisions being rolled into PM-661. What’s included (new templates): 1) Private Deployment Operator (cluster-scoped): intended for on-call/SRE-style operators to manage day-2 cluster operations without org-wide admin rights. Grants include read access to cluster state, view deployments, view runtime health, initiate safe restarts/rollouts where supported, and access operational logs/diagnostics. Explicitly excludes identity/role management, billing, org settings, and key/secret material beyond what is required for operational workflows. 2) Private Deployment Admin (environment-scoped with cluster sub-scope examples): intended for platform administrators responsible for Private environments. Grants include cluster lifecycle actions (create/upgrade/scale), configure deployment-level settings, manage routing policy artifacts tied to the environment, and manage environment-scoped retention/export configuration where applicable. Excludes org-wide identity management by default; customers can compose with Org/User Admin templates if they want a single “super-admin” role. 3) Private Read-only Auditor (environment-scoped): read-only across configuration and operational state + ability to access audit logs UI/API read endpoints. No export by default (export is a separate permission in RBAC v2 due to data exfil risk). 4) Private Audit Exporter (environment-scoped): minimal role focused on exporting audit logs (and associated export job status) without access to broader configuration changes. Intended for compliance teams / GRC / SIEM pipeline operators. 5) Private Security Auditor (environment-scoped): read access to security-relevant configuration (SSO/IdP mappings if exposed in Private control plane, KMS/HSM integration status, break-glass event summaries if surfaced), plus audit log read. Export is optional via composition with Private Audit Exporter. Scope model + examples: Templates are written to be used with RBAC v2 scopes: org -> environment -> cluster. This PR includes concrete examples showing: - A cluster-scoped binding for operators (e.g., “ops-oncall” group gets Private Deployment Operator on cluster prod-us-east-1a). - An environment-scoped binding for auditors (e.g., “grc-audit” group gets Private Read-only Auditor on environment prod). - A separate binding for SIEM export automation (e.g., “siem-exporter” service principal gets Private Audit Exporter on environment prod). Design notes / guardrails: - Separation of duties: export is separated from read by default. Review feedback requested this after seeing customers inadvertently grant export to broad read-only roles. - Avoid org-wide cluster-admin defaults: the templates are structured so that cluster mutation permissions are only present in the operator/admin templates and the documentation examples bind them to cluster or environment scopes. - Composition-friendly: customers can compose templates by assigning multiple roles to the same principal at different scopes; we intentionally avoided “one role to rule them all.” Files/areas changed (high level): - templates/private/*.yaml: new role templates for private operators/auditors/admins. - examples/private/*.yaml: example RoleBinding manifests for common IdP group mapping patterns. - docs/role-templates/private.md: brief rationale + how-to for choosing templates and scoping them. - lint/policy-lint-rules.yaml: added a rule to flag org-scope bindings for cluster-admin permissions in template examples (warn-level for now). Permissions referenced: This PR only references existing permission strings from the canonical registry (no new permissions introduced here). Notably included: audit.logs.read, audit.logs.export, private.cluster.read, private.cluster.upgrade, private.cluster.scale, private.cluster.diagnostics.read, private.runtime.logs.read, private.routing.policy.read/update (env-scoped), private.deployment.settings.read/update (env-scoped). If any of these strings change during migration, templates will be updated in lockstep (ENG-18444 tracks generator+versioning). Testing: - policy-bundle lint (naming, duplicates, forbidden org-scope examples) - template compile + snapshot test (ensures generated bundle includes new templates) - YAML schema validation for RoleTemplate and RoleBinding examples Rollout/consumers: - Redwood Private installer will start referencing these templates for bootstrap defaults in a follow-up (tracked in ENG-18410). - Console role template picker will surface these under “Private deployments” category once the console wiring lands (separate PR). Risks: - Over/under-scoping: if customers bind operator roles at environment scope, they will grant multi-cluster mutation. This PR mitigates via examples + lint warnings, but enforcement ultimately happens in the control plane’s scope evaluation. - Permission drift: if permissions are renamed/deprecated, templates must be regenerated. Follow-ups: - Add a “Private Break-glass Operator” template once break-glass flow is fully specified end-to-end (Security + SecOps). - Consider a dedicated “Private Read-only Support” role for Redwood support engineers (internal use, guarded). Review notes: Marcus requested we split audit export out of the auditor template and ensure no identity/role mutation permissions appear in the operator role. Vivek requested we include upgrade/scale actions in Private Deployment Admin and keep operator cluster-scoped by default. Colin validated the examples match how pilot customers map IdP groups and asked for a service principal example for SIEM export. All changes addressed in the final commit. Changelog: New templates are additive and do not change any existing role definitions. Existing bundles remain valid. CI: All checks green (lint, schema validate, snapshot, unit tests).

The templates are explicitly scoped (environment and cluster) and are designed to support least-privilege, separation of duties, and evidence-friendly auditability. We also saw multiple support cases where Dedicated/Private admins were blocked due to missing template permissions or where customers over-granted Org Admin to solve an operator need (SUP-48288, SUP-48219). This PR is part of ENG-18444 (role template generator + templates) and aligns with the scope model decisions being rolled into PM-661. What’s included (new templates): 1) Private Deployment Operator (cluster-scoped): intended for on-call/SRE-style operators to manage day-2 cluster operations without org-wide admin rights. Grants include read access to cluster state, view deployments, view runtime health, initiate safe restarts/rollouts where supported, and access operational logs/diagnostics. Explicitly excludes identity/role management, billing, org settings, and key/secret material beyond what is required for operational workflows. 2) Private Deployment Admin (environment-scoped with cluster sub-scope examples): intended for platform administrators responsible for Private environments. Grants include cluster lifecycle actions (create/upgrade/scale), configure deployment-level settings, manage routing policy artifacts tied to the environment, and manage environment-scoped retention/export configuration where applicable. Excludes org-wide identity management by default; customers can compose with Org/User Admin templates if they want a single “super-admin” role. 3) Private Read-only Auditor (environment-scoped): read-only across configuration and operational state + ability to access audit logs UI/API read endpoints. No export by default (export is a separate permission in RBAC v2 due to data exfil risk). 4) Private Audit Exporter (environment-scoped): minimal role focused on exporting audit logs (and associated export job status) without access to broader configuration changes. Intended for compliance teams / GRC / SIEM pipeline operators. 5) Private Security Auditor (environment-scoped): read access to security-relevant configuration (SSO/IdP mappings if exposed in Private control plane, KMS/HSM integration status, break-glass event summaries if surfaced), plus audit log read. This PR includes concrete examples showing: - A cluster-scoped binding for operators (e.g., “ops-oncall” group gets Private Deployment Operator on cluster prod-us-east-1a). - An environment-scoped binding for auditors (e.g., “grc-audit” group gets Private Read-only Auditor on environment prod). - A separate binding for SIEM export automation (e.g., “siem-exporter” service principal gets Private Audit Exporter on environment prod). Design notes / guardrails: - Separation of duties: export is separated from read by default. Review feedback requested this after seeing customers inadvertently grant export to broad read-only roles. - Avoid org-wide cluster-admin defaults: the templates are structured so that cluster mutation permissions are only present in the operator/admin templates and the documentation examples bind them to cluster or environment scopes. - Composition-friendly: customers can compose templates by assigning multiple roles to the same principal at different scopes; we intentionally avoided “one role to rule them all.” Files/areas changed (high level): - templates/private/*.yaml: new role templates for private operators/auditors/admins. - examples/private/*.yaml: example RoleBinding manifests for common IdP group mapping patterns. - docs/role-templates/private.md: brief rationale + how-to for choosing templates and scoping them. - lint/policy-lint-rules.yaml: added a rule to flag org-scope bindings for cluster-admin permissions in template examples (warn-level for now). Permissions referenced: This PR only references existing permission strings from the canonical registry (no new permissions introduced here). Notably included: audit.logs.read, audit.logs.export, private.cluster.read, private.cluster.upgrade, private.cluster.scale, private.cluster.diagnostics.read, private.runtime.logs.read, private.routing.policy.read/update (env-scoped), private.deployment.settings.read/update (env-scoped). If any of these strings change during migration, templates will be updated in lockstep (ENG-18444 tracks generator+versioning). Testing: - policy-bundle lint (naming, duplicates, forbidden org-scope examples) - template compile + snapshot test (ensures generated bundle includes new templates) - YAML schema validation for RoleTemplate and RoleBinding examples Rollout/consumers: - Redwood Private installer will start referencing these templates for bootstrap defaults in a follow-up (tracked in ENG-18410). - Console role template picker will surface these under “Private deployments” category once the console wiring lands (separate PR). Risks: - Over/under-scoping: if customers bind operator roles at environment scope, they will grant multi-cluster mutation. This PR mitigates via examples + lint warnings, but enforcement ultimately happens in the control plane’s scope evaluation. - Permission drift: if permissions are renamed/deprecated, templates must be regenerated. Follow-ups: - Add a “Private Break-glass Operator” template once break-glass flow is fully specified end-to-end (Security + SecOps). - Consider a dedicated “Private Read-only Support” role for Redwood support engineers (internal use, guarded). Review notes: Marcus requested we split audit export out of the auditor template and ensure no identity/role mutation permissions appear in the operator role. Vivek requested we include upgrade/scale actions in Private Deployment Admin and keep operator cluster-scoped by default. Colin validated the examples match how pilot customers map IdP groups and asked for a service principal example for SIEM export. All changes addressed in the final commit. Changelog: New templates are additive and do not change any existing role definitions. Existing bundles remain valid. CI: All checks green (lint, schema validate, snapshot, unit tests).

title: Add rollout-approver role + permissions for Hosted canary rollouts
preview:
Summary: Introduces a dedicated `rollout-approver` role and updates permission mappings to support the Hosted canary rollout workflow GA. This role is intended for orgs that want separation of duties: engineers can draft/create rollouts, but a smaller set of users must approve step changes (or rollback) when approval gates are enabled by policy.

Context / why: The canary workflow adds new actions that need explicit RBAC coverage: create rollout, start rollout, progress step (increase canary %), approve gated step, pause, rollback/cancel. Product + Security requested an explicit approver role rather than overloading existing admin roles, and Support requested clearer error messages when customers lack permissions.

What’s in this PR: Adds new role definition `rollout_approver` (exposed as `rollout-approver` in Console) and wires the following permissions: - `rollouts.read`: view rollout objects + timeline - `rollouts.approve`: approve a gated step (e.g., 5% → 25%) - `rollouts.pause`: pause a running rollout - `rollouts.rollback`: rollback/cancel a rollout - `routes.read`: read Hosted route config required for approval screens (baseline/canary model versions, regions)

Explicitly NOT granted to rollout-approver: - `rollouts.create` (drafting new rollouts stays with engineers) - `rollouts.start` (starting runs stays with engineers unless org chooses to also grant) - `rollouts.update_schedule` (changing the traffic plan / soak times is treated as a config change)

Default role mapping notes: - No default assignment changes for existing tenants. - `org-admin` continues to imply all rollout permissions (including approve) for backward compatibility. - `console-editor` (where used) remains able to create/start rollouts but does NOT imply approve/rollback.

Policy implementation details: - Adds action aliases used by Console/Control Plane: `rollouts:approve_step`, `rollouts:pause`, `rollouts:rollback`. - Adds resource scoping to `hosted_route` and `rollout` resources. - Ensures all write actions require `tenant_id` match and `environment` match (no cross-env leakage between staging/prod tenants).

Testing: - Updated policy unit tests for role resolution and action coverage. - Added regression test ensuring `rollout-approver` cannot create/start or edit schedules.

Rollout / compatibility: Control Plane will start emitting permission checks for these actions behind feature flag. Console will surface "You need Rollout Approver permissions" if missing. This PR is safe to land ahead of feature enablement.

References: Linear PM-1851 (guardrails + approvals), ENG-9146 (policy engine guardrails), ENG-9171 (audit events). Support context: SUP-48211 (customer asking how to do percentage rollout + approvals).

Checklist: - [x] Role name reviewed with Product Security - [x] Confirmed org-admin remains superset - [x] Added tests for deny cases - [x] Docs snippet added to policy README (role + permissions)

Notes for reviewers: Please sanity check the deny semantics for rollback. We intentionally allow rollback for rollout-approver because it’s a safety action; if you disagree, call it out and we can require both `rollouts.approve` + `rollouts.rollback` or restrict rollback to org-admin only.
text:
Summary: Introduces a dedicated `rollout-approver` role and updates permission mappings to support the Hosted canary rollout workflow GA. This role is intended for orgs that want separation of duties: engineers can draft/create rollouts, but a smaller set of users must approve step changes (or rollback) when approval gates are enabled by policy.

Context / why: The canary workflow adds new actions that need explicit RBAC coverage: create rollout, start rollout, progress step (increase canary %), approve gated step, pause, rollback/cancel. Product + Security requested an explicit approver role rather than overloading existing admin roles, and Support requested clearer error messages when customers lack permissions.

What’s in this PR: Adds new role definition `rollout_approver` (exposed as `rollout-approver` in Console) and wires the following permissions: - `rollouts.read`: view rollout objects + timeline - `rollouts.approve`: approve a gated step (e.g., 5% → 25%) - `rollouts.pause`: pause a running rollout - `rollouts.rollback`: rollback/cancel a rollout - `routes.read`: read Hosted route config required for approval screens (baseline/canary model versions, regions)

Explicitly NOT granted to rollout-approver: - `rollouts.create` (drafting new rollouts stays with engineers) - `rollouts.start` (starting runs stays with engineers unless org chooses to also grant) - `rollouts.update_schedule` (changing the traffic plan / soak times is treated as a config change)

Default role mapping notes: - No default assignment changes for existing tenants. - `org-admin` continues to imply all rollout permissions (including approve) for backward compatibility. - `console-editor` (where used) remains able to create/start rollouts but does NOT imply approve/rollback.

Policy implementation details: - Adds action aliases used by Console/Control Plane: `rollouts:approve_step`, `rollouts:pause`, `rollouts:rollback`. - Adds resource scoping to `hosted_route` and `rollout` resources. - Ensures all write actions require `tenant_id` match and `environment` match (no cross-env leakage between staging/prod tenants).

Testing: - Updated policy unit tests for role resolution and action coverage. - Added regression test ensuring `rollout-approver` cannot create/start or edit schedules.

Rollout / compatibility: Control Plane will start emitting permission checks for these actions behind feature flag. Console will surface "You need Rollout Approver permissions" if missing. This PR is safe to land ahead of feature enablement.

References: Linear PM-1851 (guardrails + approvals), ENG-9146 (policy engine guardrails), ENG-9171 (audit events). Support context: SUP-48211 (customer asking how to do percentage rollout + approvals).

Checklist: - [x] Role name reviewed with Product Security - [x] Confirmed org-admin remains superset - [x] Added tests for deny cases - [x] Docs snippet added to policy README (role + permissions)

Notes for reviewers: Please sanity check the deny semantics for rollback. We intentionally allow rollback for rollout-approver because it’s a safety action; if you disagree, call it out and we can require both `rollouts.approve` + `rollouts.rollback` or restrict rollback to org-admin only.

title: Generate built-in role templates from YAML + linting + versioned release artifacts
preview: Context: As part of RBAC v2 we want built-in role templates (Org Admin, Security Admin, Read-only Auditor, Deployment Operator, etc.) to be (a) human-reviewable, (b) lintable in CI, and (c) published as versioned artifacts that services (Control Plane, Console, Private installer) can consume deterministically. This PR introduces a YAML-first source of truth under policy_bundle/roles/*.yaml and a generator that produces the canonical JSON bundle consumed by platform components. It also adds a lint/validation suite to enforce naming/versioning conventions, uniqueness constraints, and cross-scope safety checks, and formalizes an artifact versioning scheme tied to the repo release version. Summary of changes: (1) New role template YAML schema (name, description, scopes, included_permissions, excluded_permissions, constraints) with examples for common templates; (2) Generator CLI (scripts/generate_role_templates) that outputs dist/role_templates.json and dist/role_templates.min.json with stable ordering and content hashes; (3) Lint rules: permission string format + deprecation markers, role name uniqueness, duplicate permissions, forbidden permission combos for auditor roles, and scope validation (org/project/environment/cluster); (4) Release artifact scheme: dist/ artifacts include a bundle_version (semver) and policy_bundle_revision (git SHA) and we tag releases using vX.Y.Z; consumers pin either semver tag or SHA for deterministic rollouts; (5) CI updates: add "rbac-policy-lint" and "rbac-policy-generate" checks; block merge if generated output is out of date; (6) Docs: CONTRIBUTING notes for adding/modifying templates and guidance on bumping bundle versions. Follow-ups: We will add Private-specific operator roles in pr-201 and wire consumption into redwood monorepo (Control Plane + Console) behind RBAC v2 feature flags.

This PR introduces a YAML-first source of truth under policy_bundle/roles/*.yaml and a generator that produces the canonical JSON bundle consumed by platform components. It also adds a lint/validation suite to enforce naming/versioning conventions, uniqueness constraints, and cross-scope safety checks, and formalizes an artifact versioning scheme tied to the repo release version.
